step_by_step_thinking:
Muscles involved in swallowing processes typically require intact neural control from multiple sources, including cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and sometimes XI (accessory) depending on specific roles within the mechanism. The stylopharyngeus muscle stands out here because it exclusively relies on cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) for its motor innervation. Given that the question asks about which muscles remain functional after a complete transection of the cranial part of the accessory nerve, we need to focus specifically on those whose motor innervation does not depend solely on cranial nerve XI post-transection.
In summary, since the stylopharygeous muscle's motor innervation comes entirely from cranial nerve IX, and thus would still retain functionality even if the cranial aspect of the accessory nerve were completely severed.
\boxed{C} Based on the evidence showing exclusive reliance of the stylopharyngeus muscle on cranial nerve IX for motor innervation, this muscle will remain functional regardless of damage to the cranial part of the accessory nerve.
raw_response:
Muscles involved in swallowing processes typically require intact neural control from multiple sources, including cranial nerves IX (glossopharyngeal), X (vagus), and sometimes XI (accessory) depending on specific roles within the mechanism. The stylopharyngeus muscle stands out here because it exclusively relies on cranial nerve IX (glossopharyngeal) for its motor innervation. Given that the question asks about which muscles remain functional after a complete transection of the cranial part of the accessory nerve, we need to focus specifically on those whose motor innervation does not depend solely on cranial nerve XI post-transection.
In summary, since the stylopharygeous muscle's motor innervation comes entirely from cranial nerve IX, and thus would still retain functionality even if the cranial aspect of the accessory nerve were completely severed.
\boxed{C} Based on the evidence showing exclusive reliance of the stylopharyngeus muscle on cranial nerve IX for motor innervation, this muscle will remain functional regardless of damage to the cranial part of the accessory nerve.
\boxed{C}
